claim: The high word-initial glyph constraint ({o,c,q,s,d} dominating word starts) and word-final glyph constraint ({y,n,l,r,o} dominating word ends) are artifacts of a Vigenere-like polyalphabetic cipher where the key resets at word boundaries, causing the first and last characters of each word to be systematically drawn from whichever cipher-alphabet rows correspond to the key's initial and terminal positions.
evidence_for: Positional glyph constraints are among the strongest structural signals in the corpus (belief 0.973)
A word-boundary key-reset in a polyalphabetic cipher would mechanically confine word-edge glyphs to a small subset of the cipher alphabet
The specific initial set {o,c,q,s,d} and final set {y,n,l,r,o} are each approximately 5 glyphs from a 25-glyph alphabet (~20%), consistent with 1â€“2 active key rows at word edges
This model is consistent with Currier A/B differences if the two scribes used different key lengths or starting positions
evidence_against: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is relatively high for a polyalphabetic cipher with only 25 symbols, unless the key is long
Simple Vigenere on Latin would be expected to produce entropy closer to 4.0 bits; the slight deficit may require additional explanation
Word-boundary key resets are an unusual feature not documented in known period ciphers, reducing prior plausibility
test: Model the corpus as a word-boundary-reset polyalphabetic cipher: for each word-length class (3â€“8 glyphs), compute the empirical glyph probability distribution at each within-word position and test whether position 1 and position N distributions are significantly more concentrated (lower entropy) than positions 2 through N-1 using a chi-squared test.
test_metric: Per-position glyph entropy H(glyph | position_index) across word-length-controlled bins, with particular focus on the entropy gap between edge positions and interior positions
test_threshold: Confirm if mean edge-position entropy is at least 0.4 bits lower than mean interior-position entropy; deny if edge and interior entropies differ by less than 0.15 bits

reasoning: A polyalphabetic cipher with word-boundary key resets produces a specific, testable signature: edge positions should show substantially lower glyph entropy than interior positions, and this pattern should be consistent across word lengths.
